title: AP 物理C电磁 2025年样题1

questions.prompt: A negatively charged particle is moving toward the top of the page when it enters a region of space with a uniform magnetic field B→ directed to the right, as shown in the figure. In what direction does the magnetic field exert a force on the particle immediately after the particle enters the region?

options.content: To the right
To the left
Out of the page
Into the page

questions.explanation: Answer key not available yet for this imported exam.

questions.prompt:
A parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates is charged and then disconnected from the source of emf. If the space between the plates is now filled with a dielectric material, what happens to the capacitance of the capacitor and the magnitude of the electric field between the plates?

CapacitanceMagnitude of the Electric Field
AIncreasesIncreases
BIncreasesDecreases
CDecreasesIncreases
DDecreasesDecreases

options.content: A
B
C
D

questions.explanation: Answer key not available yet for this imported exam.

questions.prompt: An electric field E→ within a region of space is represented by the arrows shown in the figure. The dashed-line curves represent equipotential lines for three electric potentials, VA, VB, and VC. An electron is released from rest at Point P on the VB equipotential. Which of the following correctly indicates the resulting motion of the electron and provides a valid justification?

options.content: The electron will move toward the VC equipotential because electrons move toward stronger electric fields.
The electron will move toward the VC equipotential because electrons experience an electric force in the direction of the electric field.
The electron will move toward the VA equipotential because electrons move toward lower electric potentials.
The electron will move toward the VA equipotential because electrons always accelerate toward higher potential.

questions.prompt: The current in a resistor of resistance R0 varies with time t. Starting at t=0, the current in the resistor is I(t)=αt, where α is a positive constant. How much energy is dissipated by the resistor during the time interval 0≤t≤tf ?

options.content: αR0tf
α2R0tf2
2α2R0tf
13α2R0tf3

questions.prompt: A square conducting loop of wire is initially in the xy-plane with its center located at the origin. There is a magnetic field that is uniform in space, but not necessarily constant in time, that is initially described by B→=B0(i^+k^) at time t=0. Which of the following would cause an emf to be induced in the loop of wire?

options.content: Varying the field in time as B→(t)=B0(i^cos(ωt)+k^) while keeping the loop stationary.
Varying the field in time as B→(t)=B0(i^+k^cos(ωt)) while keeping the loop stationary.
Rotating the loop about the z-axis while keeping the field constant in time.
Moving the loop in the positive z-direction while keeping the field constant in time.

questions.explanation: Answer key not available yet for this imported exam.

questions.prompt:
Two concentric spherical conducting shells and four labeled points R, S, T, and U are shown in the figure. The outer shell has a net charge of −20nC. The inner shell has a net charge of +10nC.

What is the charge on the outer surface of the outer shell?

options.content: −30nC
−20nC
−10nC
Zero

questions.prompt:
Two concentric spherical conducting shells and four labeled points R, S, T, and U are shown in the figure. The outer shell has a net charge of −20nC. The inner shell has a net charge of +10nC.

The magnitudes of the electric fields at points R, S, T, and U are ER, ES, ET, and EU, respectively. Which of the following correctly compares the magnitudes of the electric fields?

options.content: ES>ET>(ER=EU)
(ES=ET)>EU>ER
(ES=ET)>(ER=EU)
ET>ES>ER>EU

questions.prompt: A long conducting cylinder has radius R, as shown in the figure, and carries a current to the left. The current density varies with distance r from the cylinder’s central axis according to the equation J=kr2, where r≤R and k is a positive constant. What is the total current in the cylinder?

options.content: 13kR3
14kR4
π2kR4
π5kR5

questions.prompt: A ferromagnetic sample of metal is held in a strong external magnetic field created by a nearby magnet for a long time. The magnet is then moved far away from the sample. Which of the following correctly describes the magnetization of the sample after the nearby magnet is removed, and explains why?

options.content: The sample is magnetized. The magnetic domains of the sample will become aligned with the external field and will remain in that alignment after the sample is removed from the external field.
The sample is magnetized. Free electrons in the sample will become polarized in the presence of the external field and will remain polarized after the sample is removed from the external field.
The sample is unmagnetized. The sample will have an equal number of magnetic dipoles aligned with the north and south poles of the nearby magnet.

The sample is unmagnetized. After becoming aligned when the magnet was nearby, the atoms’ magnetic dipoles in the sample return to being randomly oriented when the external field is no longer present.

options.content: kQ2(1x0+1x0+L)
kQLln(x0+Lx0)
kQLln(Lx0)
kQx0

Magnitude of B→Direction of B→

questions.prompt: A thin conducting wire is located along the central axis of a long conducting cylindrical shell of radius 3.0mm, as shown in the figure. The central wire is carrying a current of 1.0A toward the left, and the cylindrical shell is carrying a current of 1.0A that is toward the right and uniformly distributed around the shell. If the magnetic field has a magnitude B1 at a distance of 1.0mm from the central axis and a magnitude B2 at distance of 2.0mm from the central axis, what is the ratio B2B1?

options.content: 14
12
11
The ratio is undefined because B1 and B2 are both zero.

questions.prompt: A positive point charge +q is located to the left of a hollow conducting shell, as shown in the figure. There is zero net charge on the shell. Which of the following indicates the direction, if any, of the electric field at Point P at the center of the conducting shell and provides a valid justification?

options.content: The field is zero and has no direction. The electric potential is the same everywhere inside of a conducting shell.
The field is zero and has no direction. At the center of a closed, conducting shell, the field created by uniform charge distributions on the shell’s inner and outer surfaces must be zero.
The field direction is toward the right. In the nonconducting region inside a conducting shell, the field is that of any external charge or charge distribution.

The field direction is toward the right. Within a conducting shell, charge distributed on the surface of the shell reduces, but does not cancel, the field from any external charge or charge distribution.

questions.prompt: Point C is located at the center of a circle, which is represented by the dashed line in the figure. Along the top half of the circle is a thin, semicircular ring made of an insulating material containing a uniformly distributed positive charge +Q. A point charge of the same total charge +Q is located at the bottommost point on the circle. Which of the following correctly indicated the direction, if any, of the electric field E→C at Point C and provides a valid justification?

options.content: E→C is zero and has no direction. The semicircle has equal amounts of charge in the left half and the right half.
E→C is zero and has no direction. There are equal amounts of charge above and below Point C, all at the same distance from Point C.
E→C is directed toward the top of the figure. The electric field due to the ring is zero at Point C, so only the point charge contributes to the field.
E→C is nonzero and directed toward the top of the figure. The electric field at Point C due to the ring is nonzero but less than that due to the point charge.
